subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define countable and uncountable sets. Prove that the set of rational numbers is countable.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Using Venn diagram, prove: A ∩ (B ∪ C) = (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C)

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define relation. Explain equivalence relation and partial ordering relation with suitable examples.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Let A = {1, 2, 3}, R = {(1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (1,2), (2,1)}. Check whether R is an equivalence relation and find equivalence classes.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define one-one, onto and bijective functions. Prove that inverse of a function exists if and only if the function is bijective.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: State and prove Pigeonhole Principle.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Construct truth table for the proposition (p → q) ↔ (¬p ∨ q) and show that it is a tautology.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Convert the following statement into predicate logic and write its negation: "Every student studies Discrete Mathematics."

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Prove by mathematical induction that: 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + n = n(n+1)/2

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Prove by contradiction that √2 is irrational.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define group and Abelian group. Prove that identity element of a group is unique.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Find all subgroups of the group Z_8.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define Finite State Machine. Explain FSM as a language recognizer.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Design a finite state machine that accepts all binary strings ending with 01.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define Euler path and Euler circuit. State necessary and sufficient conditions for their existence.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Explain Dijkstra's Algorithm and find the shortest path from a given source vertex 0 to all other vertices in a weighted graph. [Image: A weighted undirected graph with 5 vertices labeled 0 to 4. The edges and their weights are: edge between 0 and 1 with weight 4, edge between 0 and 2 with weight 8, edge between 1 and 4 with weight 6, edge between 2 and 3 with weight 2, and edge between 3 and 4 with weight 10]

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Consider three sets A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, B = {3, 4, 5, 6} and C = {1, 5, 7}. Using Venn diagrams, determine and shade the regions corresponding to (A ∪ B) ∩ (B ∩ C) and A ∩ (B ∪ C). Verify if these expressions are equivalent by listing the elements.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Define and illustrate an equivalence relation by constructing an equivalence relation on the set S = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} where two elements are related if they have the same remainder when divided by 3.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: What is pigeonhole principle? Prove it by using mathematical induction and use it to show that in a group of 15 people, at least two share the same birth month.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Explain a recursively defined function. Solve the recurrence relation f(n) = f(n-1) + 3 with initial condition f(0) = 2.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Prove that the set G = {0, 1, 2, 3} under addition modulo 4 is an abelian group. Identify the identity and inverse elements.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Define a homomorphism between two groups. Verify whether the mapping f: R → R defined by f(x) = 2x is a homomorphism under addition.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Consider the propositions p, q and r, where: p: "It is raining." q: "I am carrying an umbrella." r: "I stay dry." Assume the implication (p → q) → r. Construct a truth table for the expression (p → q) → r and determine if it is a tautology or contradiction.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: What are predicates in propositional logic? Define universal and existential quantifiers with examples.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define graph theory and explain the basic terminology of graphs such as vertices, edges, degree and adjacency.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Define and give an example of an isomorphic graph pair. Verify isomorphism between two adjacency matrices.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: For the graph G with vertices V = {A, B, C, D} and edges E = {(A,B), (B,C), (C,D), (D,A), (A,C)}, determine: i) Whether the graph contains a Eulerian path or circuit or not. ii) All Hamiltonian circuits in the graph G.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: How many permutations can be formed from the letters of the word "MATHEMATICS" such that all vowels are together?

subQuestions.label: a)

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Define planar graph. Prove that for any connected planar graph, v - e + r = 2 Where v, e, r is the number of vertices, edges, and regions of the graph respectively.

subQuestions.text: Find the numbers between 1 to 500 that are not divisible by any of the integers 2 or 3 or 5 or 7.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: A collection of 10 electric bulbs contain 3 defective ones. (i) In how many ways can a sample of four bulbs be selected? (ii) In how many ways can a sample of 4 bulbs be selected which contain 2 good bulbs and 2 defective ones? (iii) In how many ways can a sample of 4 bulbs be selected so that either the sample contains 3 good ones and 1 defectives ones or 1 good and 3 defectives ones?

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Discuss about Complete digraph and Euler Graph with the help of suitable examples.

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Solve the following recurrence equation using generating function. G(K) - 7G(K-1) + 10G(K-2) = 8K + 6.

subQuestions.label: b)
subQuestions.text: Prove the validity of the following argument "if the races are fixed so the casinos are crooked, then the tourist trade will decline. If the tourist trade decreases, then the police will be happy. The police force is never happy. Therefore, the races are not fixed."

subQuestions.label: a)

subQuestions.label: a)
subQuestions.text: Out of 120 students surveyed, it was found that 20 students have studied French, 50 students have studied English, 70 students have studied Hindi, 5 have studied English and French, 20 have studied English and Hindi, 10 have studied Hindi and French, only 3 students have studied all the three languages. Find how many students have studied: i) Hindi alone ii) French alone iii) English, but not Hindi iv) Hindi, but not French

subQuestions.label: b)

subQuestions.label: a)

subQuestions.latex: (\sim (p \lor (q \lor r)) \Leftrightarrow ((p \lor q) \land (p \lor r))), ((\sim q \Rightarrow \sim p) \Rightarrow (p \Rightarrow q))

questions.text: Let S = N × N. Let * be the operation on S defined by (a, b) * (a', b') = (aa', bb').

subQuestions.label: i)
subQuestions.text: Define f : (S, *) → (Q, ×) by f(a, b) = a/b. Show that f is a homomorphism.

subQuestions.label: ii)
subQuestions.text: Find the congruence relation ~ in S determined by the homomorphism f, that is, where x ~ y if f(x) = f(y).
